item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is less of an anomaly and more of an actual type of person, which I don't think belongs on this site personally. Maybe that's the point and I'm just a boomer who can't appreciate more symbolic anomalies, but I feel like the symbolism could've been executed in a fashion where it would make sense as an anomaly as well as a socially distant type of person.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think, conceptually, this is an idea with great potential — and that the structure you've set up here (with the punchline being the Foundation's disinterest) is solid. Execution-wise, though, I feel like this is missing something.

There's an element of self-indulgence here (not in the sense of you, the author, indulging in yourself — but in the way this anomaly 'confirms' some of our deepest insecurities as both true and anomalous), and I feel like there are some ways you could have side-stepped that. One solution might have been to make the anomaly a single person rather than a group. That way, you could dig deeper into the idea and humanize it — and avoid that 'self-indulgence' aspect.

Either way, I don't think this article is bad! Not by any stretch of the imagination. It just feels like a missed opportunity, to me.

ETA: An idea that's very similar in scope (and an example of how I think this could have worked better) is Weryllium's article, SCP-3949. Not the same premise, but a very similar structure (and I think the reason it works in this case is because it gets specific rather than applying this idea more generally).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love your stuff Tan, but unfortunately this doesn't work for me, in the sense that it doesn't hit me the way it should.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really funny. I'm sure there's been a -j similar to this about how most people do jackshit in their lives and are ultimately forgettable, but I think this works. +1

All can be good in the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mfw when I'm an SCP :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Me: Idea seems trite, but the punchline will probably be a clever turn on how useful InvisiHumans are for espionage and reality-bending interac —

Entry Punchline: Silly sad joke.

Me: ok.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your feedback. Fortunately, however, there are no shortage of slots!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm legitimately not sure how to feel about this article. I don't think it needs to be removed, but if it were, I wouldn't care at all. If you were trying to make the article feel empty and meaningless, great job!.. if not… you should probably add more, anything to the article. Ultimately, I'd rather see someone else use this slot for something better. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm very surprised nobody really mentioned Joe Schmo here, because it seems like those two concepts cover a similar topic with pretty similar means. But unfortunately, while Joe seems like an interesting anomaly that illustrates the social struggles of a certain type of individuals, this SCP just reads like a lazy attempt to capitalize on negative emotions some people experience and pass it over as good writing. I'm not a huge fan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This gave me vibes from the Nobody GoI. I was surprised when there wasn't a tag for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thirding this. It's so unsubtle that it feels lazy - "hey, you know that relatable insecurity you have about the world? Here it is with an SCP number.

…That's it."

It's like the equivalent of writing up pop culture characters as SCPs but for emotions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now this is modern horror

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm…kinda torn on this a little. Conceptually, it's really fun and there's some really good emotional stuff here, but it feels too much like the start of an article and not a full one: it really feels like it doesn't quite end on enough of a shock to work.

It…also feels like it's a little too similar to SCP-4857 for my liking; only this doesn't go anywhere near as far into exploring the concept?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the concept, but it's a bit boring… idk, no vote for now

This honestly doesn't feel like it fits as an anomaly, it feels more like "what if a thing in real life actually had an anomalous explanation". I would give this a -1, but I don't hate this document so I think I may just stick to a neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel attacked, sir/madam. You have wounded my soul.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Going to echo this opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I can count myself as an SCP-5190.
I'm just glad it's not my fault I'm so boring now! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Welp, that made evoked my anxiety.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, I didn't know I was an instance of SCP-5190. Neat.

Anyways, this is almost unbearably sad. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was quite an entertaining read ngl. The concept about us forgetting other people is already quite an interesting and a mildly sad one, so this putting it into action is quite nice. I can definitely see where some are coming from in terms of the predictability of the plot, though for some weird reason I didn't spot the ending from a mile away tbh lol.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Instances of SCP-5190 will make little to no significant impact to the world outside them throughout their lives.

I knew exactly where this was going after this, and nothing along the way surprised or entertained me. The stinger was immensely predictable and didn't do anything for me because of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, background people.

This is unpleasantly narcissistic and not in a way that really develops that as horror. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This does absolutely nothing for me. This same type of punchline feels like it's been done better in a dozen other SCPs, and on top of that, it's about as subtle with its brand of misery as an orbital brick to the head. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was a great gut punch at the end. Also, :( +1
i am now sad. why would you do this.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Thanks to Etoile, dave and jackalrelated for their valuable feedback!
